prompt: Maya in her parked car, engine off, for once not reaching for her phone, first honey light through the windshield finding one cheek.
Maya at the kitchen counter at 6:50am, toast going cold beside her, eyes fixed on the middle distance while the morning happens around her — the autopilot Tuesday, seen.
Maya's blurred evening scroll — her face lit only by phone glow on a dark sofa, the room and its one warm lamp soft and unnoticed behind her, the evening passing without her.

The wide polished gold Pulse band resting on near-black honed stone, a single warm rim light tracing its edge out of deep shadow, macro jewelry photography, medium-format, faint gold reflection pooling beneath, everything else void. One soft concentric ripple of light suggested in the reflection beneath the band — a pulse, not a ping; nothing on it to read, nothing on it to check.
A cooling cup of coffee by a sunlit kitchen window, steam still rising, a resting hand beside it wearing the wide gold band — someone's ordinary Tuesday, quietly changed.
Breath fogging a cold windowpane at dawn, a fingertip drawing one short upward line through the bloom.
The gold ring beside a folded tape measure and a glass of water on cream linen, clear even daylight — every practical question laid out calmly on the table.
Maya paused in a doorway in late-afternoon light, hand resting on the frame, actually looking at her own living room as if seeing it for the first time.
An empty chair by a bright window, a folded blanket over its arm, late-afternoon light moving slowly across the floor — nothing here is in a hurry.

Opened box on a windowsill in early-morning light, tissue folded back, ring catching its first real sun, lime-lit leaf edge on the sill plant. No hands — the moment before.

An unopened box on a hallway table in cool morning light, one warm sidelight just arriving across it from a doorway. Someone thought of you.
Maya by the bathroom window in first light, sliding the gold band onto her finger — no ceremony, just the ring going on with the rest of the day.
Maya stopped mid-task at the lamp-lit sink, one wet hand still on a bowl, small surprised stillness, thumb finding the ring without looking. Clearest, sharpest light of the set.
The gold ring resting on its small ceramic dish beside a neatly made bed, soft mid-morning light, everything unhurried — it will keep.

Maya slumped in a sunlit corner chair, eyes closed, jaw unclenched, one open hand on her chest. The exhale she didn't know she was holding.
Maya kneeling at a child's eye level at the front door, backpack still on her shoulder, actually listening. Clean confident daylight.
Warm practical-lit dinner, Maya and her daughter close in a two-shot, real food and a little mess, Maya leaning in and present.
Macro: hand flat over the heart on bare skin, low sun raking, the pulse at the wrist catching light, the warm gold ring on the same hand.
Maya settled into the sofa on a Sunday evening, lamp just switched on, tea steaming on the side table, the week set down — dusk blue in the window, gold in the room.
Three handwritten notes fanned on cream linen in window light, different inks and different hands, a pen resting nearby — small true things people noticed this week.
The ring unworn on a bedside dish in cool grey-blue morning light, near-monochrome — one thin blade of warm amber entering from the frame edge, just reaching the band.
Bare hands cupped around a warm mug in cool early light, no ring anywhere, one modest band of gold sun crossing the table toward them — practice first, product later.
Maya at the table watching her daughter laugh, her own eyes wet, hand pressed to her mouth — caught by how much she almost missed.
